items.title: Mechanism of Water Reabsorption in the Kidney
items.content: The kidney modulates water reabsorption through AVP acting on V3-type receptors, increasing cyclic AMP levels and activating PKA-dependent phosphorylation. This leads to aquaporin-2 channel insertion into principal cells' membranes in the collecting duct, facilitating transepithelial water absorption down the medullary osmotic gradient.
keyPoints: AVP acts on V3-type receptors in the thick ascending limb of Henle and principal cells of the collecting duct.
PKA-dependent phosphorylation activates Na+-Cl- and K+ transport, crucial for countercurrent mechanism.

Aquaporin-2 channel insertion into principal cell membranes under AVP influence allows water absorption, producing concentrated urine (up to 1200 mOsm/kg) or dilute urine (30–50 mOsm/kg) in its absence.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances (part 1)

items.title: Regulation of Sodium Homeostasis
items.content: Sodium homeostasis is critical for maintaining arterial circulatory integrity, with the Na+/K+-ATPase pump ensuring most body sodium remains extracellular. Renal reabsorption and excretion of Na+ regulate ECF volume, affecting systemic arterial resistance.
keyPoints: 85–90% of body Na+ is extracellular due to the Na+/K+-ATPase membrane pump.
ECF volume depends on total-body Na+ content; renal Na+ retention/excretion modulates this.
At a GFR of 180 L/d and serum Na+ ~140 mM, kidneys filter about 25,200 mmol/d of Na+, requiring reabsorption of 99.6% to maintain balance.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances (part 1)

items.title: Hormonal Regulation of Renal Sodium Reabsorption
items.content: Renal sodium reabsorption is finely regulated by hormones such as angiotensin II, which enhances proximal Na+-Cl- reabsorption, and aldosterone, which activates ENaC channels in the distal nephron for Na+ absorption and K+ excretion.
keyPoints: Angiotensin II and adrenergic receptors increase proximal Na+-Cl- reabsorption under renal sympathetic influence.
Aldosterone acts on the aldosterone-sensitive distal nephron, particularly activating ENaC channels in principal cells for Na+ absorption.
Dopamine has a natriuretic effect, contrasting with angiotensin II and adrenergic influences.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances (part 1)

items.title: Neurohumoral Response to Arterial Underfilling
items.content: Arterial underfilling, sensed by ventricular and vascular pressure receptors, triggers neurohumoral activation aimed at maintaining circulatory integrity. This involves increased sympathetic tone, activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis, and elevated circulating AVP levels.
keyPoints: Neurohumoral activation includes increased sympathetic tone, renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis activation, and increased circulating AVP.
AVP induces vasoconstriction, increases sympathetic nervous system tone, enhances renal retention of water and Na⁺-Cl⁻, and modulates the arterial baroreceptor reflex.
These responses are crucial in conditions like hypovolemic states, low-output cardiac failure, decreased oncotic pressure, and increased capillary permeability.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances (part 2)

items.title: Role of AVP in Circulatory Integrity
items.content: AVP plays a critical role in defending circulatory integrity by inducing vasoconstriction, enhancing sympathetic nervous system tone, increasing renal retention of water and Na⁺-Cl⁻, and modulating the arterial baroreceptor reflex.
keyPoints: AVP functions to maintain circulatory integrity through multiple mechanisms.
It induces vasoconstriction and increases sympathetic nervous system tone.
AVP enhances renal retention of both water and Na⁺-Cl⁻.
Modulation of the arterial baroreceptor reflex is another key function of AVP.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances (part 2)

items.title: Renal Causes of Hypovolemia
items.content: Hypovolemia can result from renal causes, including osmotic diuresis due to high filtered loads of solutes like glucose and urea, pharmacologic diuretics, or drugs affecting Na⁺-Cl⁻ reabsorption. Conditions such as mineralocorticoid deficiency or tubulointerstitial injury also contribute.
keyPoints: Osmotic diuresis occurs with high filtered loads of solutes like glucose and urea.
Pharmacologic diuretics impair Na⁺-Cl⁻ reabsorption at specific nephron sites.
Drugs such as acetazolamide, trimethoprim, and pentamidine affect Na⁺-Cl⁻ absorption.
Mineralocorticoid deficiency or resistance reduces Na⁺-Cl⁻ reabsorption in the aldosterone-sensitive distal nephron.
Tubulointerstitial injury can impair distal tubular Na⁺-Cl⁻ and/or water absorption.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPOVOLEMIA (part 1)

items.title: Extrarenal Causes of Hypovolemia
items.content: Nonrenal causes include fluid loss from the gastrointestinal tract, skin, and respiratory system. Conditions like vomiting or diarrhea alter electrolyte balance, leading to metabolic alkalosis or acidosis.
keyPoints: Gastrointestinal losses: 2 L ingested + 7 L secreted daily; 100–200 mL fecal fluid loss.
Vomiting causes metabolic alkalosis due to low pH gastric secretions.
Diarrhea leads to metabolic acidosis from high $HCO_{3}^{-}$ in biliary, pancreatic, and intestinal secretions.
Insensible losses: 500–650 mL/d from skin and respiratory tract; increased by fever or heat exposure.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPOVOLEMIA (part 1)

items.title: Diagnostic Evaluation of Hypovolemia
items.content: Diagnosis involves history, symptoms like fatigue and postural dizziness, and signs such as decreased JVP, orthostatic tachycardia (>15–20 bpm), and orthostatic hypotension (>10–20 mmHg drop). Severe cases may present with shock.
keyPoints: Symptoms: fatigue, weakness, thirst, postural dizziness, oliguria, cyanosis, abdominal/pain, confusion.
Signs of decreased ECFV: diminished skin turgor, dry oral mucous membranes.
Reliable signs: decreased JVP, orthostatic tachycardia (>15–20 bpm), orthostatic hypotension (>10–20 mmHg drop).
Severe fluid loss may lead to hypovolemic shock with hypotension, tachycardia, peripheral vasoconstriction.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPOVOLEMIA (part 1)

items.title: Interpreting Urine Electrolytes in Hypovolemia
items.content: In hypovolemic states, urine sodium concentration and osmolality provide critical insights into the underlying cause. Nonrenal causes of hypovolemia typically present with a urine Na⁺ concentration <20 mM and urine osmolality >450 mOsm/kg due to increased renal tubular reabsorption. Conversely, renal causes like acute tubular necrosis or diabetes insipidus (DI) show a urine Na⁺ concentration often >20 mM.
keyPoints: Urine Na⁺ <20 mM and osmolality >450 mOsm/kg indicate nonrenal hypovolemia.
Renal causes of hypovolemia present with urine Na⁺ >20 mM.
Urine Cl⁻ <25 mM suggests hypovolemia in cases of vomiting, diarrhea, or diuretic use.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPOVOLEMIA (part 2) | Hypovolemia

items.title: Fluid Resuscitation Strategies for Hypovolemia
items.content: The choice of fluid resuscitation in hypovolemic patients depends on the underlying pathophysiology. Isotonic saline is preferred for normonatremic or hyponatremic patients, while hypernatremic patients require hypotonic solutions. Metabolic acidosis from bicarbonate loss should be treated with intravenous bicarbonate. Severe hemorrhage necessitates red cell transfusions without exceeding a hematocrit of 35%.
keyPoints: Use isotonic saline (0.9% NaCl) for normonatremic or hyponatremic hypovolemia.
Hypernatremic patients need hypotonic solutions like 5% dextrose or diluted saline.
Intravenous bicarbonate is indicated for metabolic acidosis from bicarbonate loss.
Red cell transfusions should not increase hematocrit beyond 35% in severe hemorrhage.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPOVOLEMIA (part 2) | Hypovolemia

items.title: Understanding Hyponatremia: Role of AVP and Volume Status
items.content: Hyponatremia often results from defects in water homeostasis, particularly involving antidiuretic hormone (AVP) dysregulation. Conditions like heart failure and cirrhosis can lead to neurohumoral activation that increases AVP levels, causing water retention and hyponatremia despite a hypervolemic state.
keyPoints: Hyponatremia is primarily caused by abnormalities in water homeostasis.
AVP dysregulation plays a central role in most cases of hyponatremia.
Hypovolemia leads to increased AVP levels at each serum osmolality level.
In hypervolemic states (e.g., heart failure, cirrhosis), neurohumoral activation increases AVP, causing water retention and hyponatremia.
Absolute plasma Na⁺ concentration does not indicate volume status.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · SODIUM DISORDERS

items.title: Interpreting Sodium Levels: Importance of Volume Status
items.content: The absolute plasma sodium concentration is insufficient to determine a patient's volume status. Disorders in sodium homeostasis affect the extracellular fluid volume (ECFV) and circulatory integrity, necessitating consideration of both sodium levels and volume status for accurate diagnosis and treatment.
keyPoints: Absolute plasma Na⁺ concentration does not reflect volume status.
Sodium disorders impact ECFV and circulatory integrity.
Volume status must be considered in the diagnostic and therapeutic approach to sodium disorders.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · SODIUM DISORDERS

items.title: Understanding Hypovolemic Hyponatremia
items.content: Hypovolemic hyponatremia results from a net loss of sodium chloride and water, leading to neurohumoral activation that increases AVP. This condition is characterized by low urine Na⁺ concentration (<20 mM) unless caused by renal issues or adrenal insufficiency.
keyPoints: Plasma Na⁺ concentration <135 mM
Hypovolemic hyponatremia involves neurohumoral activation increasing AVP
Urine Na⁺ concentration typically <20 mM, except in renal causes (>20 mM)
Thiazide diuretics can mimic SIAD by causing volume depletion and polydipsia
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosing Cerebral Salt Wasting
items.content: Cerebral salt wasting is a rare cause of hypovolemic hyponatremia, characterized by inappropriate natriuresis and clinical hypovolemia due to intracranial disease. It differs from SIAD as it responds well to aggressive Na⁺-Cl⁻ repletion.
keyPoints: Cerebral salt wasting involves hyponatremia with clinical hypovolemia
Associated disorders include subarachnoid hemorrhage, traumatic brain injury, and meningitis
Distinct from SIAD due to response to Na⁺-Cl⁻ repletion
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics of Hypervolemic Hyponatremia
items.content: In hypervolemic hyponatremia, total-body Na⁺-Cl⁻ increases more than total-body water, reducing plasma Na⁺ concentration. It is associated with low urine Na⁺ concentration (<10 mM) and serves as a prognostic indicator in conditions like CHF, cirrhosis, and nephrotic syndrome.
keyPoints: Plasma Na⁺ concentration <135 mM
Total-body water increase exceeds total-body Na⁺-Cl⁻ increase
Urine Na⁺ concentration typically <10 mM
Degree of hyponatremia indicates neurohumoral activation and prognosis
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 1)

items.title: Euvolemic Hyponatremia in Secondary Adrenal Insufficiency
items.content: In secondary adrenal insufficiency, euvolemic hyponatremia occurs due to glucocorticoid deficiency. This condition affects AVP release, and hydrocortisone replacement can normalize AVP response to osmolality.
keyPoints: Secondary adrenal insufficiency leads to euvolemic hyponatremia due to glucocorticoid deficiency.
Hydrocortisone replacement normalizes AVP response to osmolality.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 2)

items.title: SIAD and Its Patterns of AVP Secretion
items.content: SIAD is the most common cause of euvolemic hyponatremia, characterized by distinct patterns of AVP secretion. These include unregulated AVP secretion, failure to suppress AVP at lower osmolalities, a reset osmostat, and undetectable circulating AVP.
keyPoints: SIAD is the most frequent cause of euvolemic hyponatremia.
Four patterns of AVP secretion in SIAD: unregulated, failure to suppress, reset osmostat, and undetectable AVP.
Gain-in-function mutations in V2 AVP receptor can lead to nephrogenic SIAD.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 2)

items.title: Hyponatremia Due to Low Solute Intake
items.content: Hyponatremia from low solute intake, such as in beer potomania, results from inadequate dietary solutes. It is characterized by very low urine osmolality and sodium concentration.
keyPoints: Low solute intake can cause hyponatremia, seen in conditions like beer potomania.
Patients present with urine osmolality <100–200 mOsm/kg and urine Na⁺ <10–20 mM.
Hyponatremia corrects promptly with saline hydration or resumption of a normal diet.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 2)

items.title: Acute Hyponatremic Encephalopathy: A Medical Emergency
items.content: Acute hyponatremia leads to rapid cerebral edema due to overwhelmed volume regulatory mechanisms, causing severe neurological symptoms and complications. It is a medical emergency requiring immediate attention.
keyPoints: Neurologic symptoms include nausea, headache, vomiting, seizure activity, brainstem herniation, coma, and death.
Normocapneic or hypercapneic respiratory failure can occur due to neurogenic pulmonary edema.
Acute symptomatic hyponatremia is more common in women before menopause.
Iatrogenic causes include hypotonic IV fluids and increased AVP postoperatively.
Exercise-associated hyponatremia linked to nonosmotic AVP increase and excessive water intake.
Recreational drugs like MDMA can induce severe acute hyponatremia.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 3)

items.title: Chronic Hyponatremia: Subtle Symptoms and Risks
items.content: Persistent chronic hyponatremia leads to efflux of organic osmolytes, reducing intracellular osmolality. Despite cellular adaptation, symptoms like vomiting, nausea, confusion, and seizures can occur at Na⁺ <125 mM.
keyPoints: Chronic hyponatremia is defined by a duration >48 hours.
Symptoms include vomiting, nausea, confusion, seizures at plasma Na⁺ <125 mM.
Even asymptomatic patients may have gait and cognitive defects.
Increased risk of falls and bony fractures due to neurologic dysfunction and reduced bone density.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 3)

items.title: Osmotic Demyelination Syndrome: Risks with Rapid Correction
items.content: Rapid correction of hyponatremia (>8–10 mM in 24 h or 18 mM in 48 h) can lead to osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS), particularly affecting the pons, due to hypertonic stress and delayed reaccumulation of osmolytes.
keyPoints: Rapid correction (>8–10 mM/24h or 18 mM/48h) risks ODS.
ODS can cause para-paresis, quadriparesis, dysphagia, dysarthria, diplopia, locked-in syndrome, and loss of consciousness.
Extrapontine myelinolysis may affect cerebellum, lateral geniculate body, thalamus, putamen, cerebral cortex or sub-cortex.
Relowering Na⁺ can prevent or attenuate ODS; risk factors include alcoholism, malnutrition, hypokalemia, liver transplantation.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 3)

items.title: Diagnostic Approach and Laboratory Evaluation in Hyponatremia
items.content: A comprehensive diagnostic approach to hyponatremia involves evaluating volume status, drug history, and potential causes of excessive AVP. Key laboratory tests include serum osmolality to rule out pseudohyponatremia and effective osmolality calculations. Additional tests assess renal function, glucose levels, uric acid concentration, and endocrine functions.
keyPoints: Serum osmolality is measured; effective osmolality <275 mOsm/kg confirms true hyponatremia.
Pseudohyponatremia occurs when plasma tonicity is normal or increased despite low Na⁺ concentration, often due to hyperlipidemia/hyperproteinemia.
Glucose-induced hyponatremia resolves with correction of hyperglycemia; plasma Na⁺ falls ~1.6–2.4 mM per 100 mg/dL increase in glucose.
Uric acid levels: <4 mg/dL suggests SIAD, while higher levels suggest volume depletion.
Endocrine tests for hypothyroidism and adrenal insufficiency are crucial; cosyntropin test assesses primary adrenal insufficiency.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 4)

items.title: Role of Urine Electrolytes in Hyponatremia Evaluation
items.content: Urine electrolyte and osmolality measurements are critical for differentiating types of hyponatremia. They help distinguish between hypovolemic, SIAD, polydipsic, and other forms based on Na⁺ concentration and urine osmolality values.
keyPoints: Urine Na⁺ <20–30 mM suggests hypovolemic hyponatremia unless a Na⁺-avid syndrome is present.
SIAD typically presents with urine Na⁺ >30 mM, but overlap exists in the elderly.
Plasma Na⁺ correction after saline hydration confirms hypovolemic hyponatremia.
Urine osmolality <100 mOsm/kg indicates polydipsia; >400 mOsm/kg suggests AVP excess.
Intermediate urine osmolality values suggest multifactorial causes.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 4)

items.title: Imaging and Special Considerations in Hyponatremia
items.content: Radiologic imaging, including chest x-ray and CT scans, may be necessary to identify pulmonary or CNS causes of hyponatremia. Thiazide-induced hyponatremia requires careful evaluation before diagnosing SIAD.
keyPoints: Screening chest x-ray may miss small-cell carcinoma; consider thoracic CT in high-risk patients (e.g., smokers).
Thiazide-associated hyponatremia can mimic SIAD; defer SIAD diagnosis until 1–2 weeks after stopping thiazides.
Urine K⁺ concentration is needed to calculate the urine-to-plasma electrolyte ratio, aiding fluid restriction response prediction.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 4)

items.title: Urgency of Correction Based on Symptoms
items.content: The urgency to correct hyponatremia is determined by the presence and severity of symptoms. Acute hyponatremia presents with severe symptoms such as seizures, requiring rapid correction, whereas chronic hyponatremia (present for >48 h) typically has less severe symptoms.
keyPoints: Acute hyponatremia can present with headache, nausea, vomiting, seizures, obtundation, and central herniation.
Chronic hyponatremia is defined as being present for more than 48 hours and generally presents with milder symptoms.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Hyponatremia

items.title: Risk of Osmotic Demyelination Syndrome (ODS) in Correction
items.content: Patients with chronic hyponatremia are at risk for osmotic demyelination syndrome if plasma sodium concentration is corrected too rapidly. The thresholds to avoid ODS are a correction rate of >8–10 mM within the first 24 hours and >18 mM within the first 48 hours.
keyPoints: ODS risk arises with rapid correction in chronic hyponatremia.
Correction should not exceed 8-10 mM in the first 24 hours or 18 mM in the first 48 hours.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Hyponatremia

items.title: Fluid Restriction and Electrolyte Management
items.content: In chronic hyponatremia, fluid restriction is crucial. The urine-to-plasma electrolyte ratio helps guide the degree of restriction: a ratio >1 suggests aggressive restriction (<500 mL/d), ~1 suggests 500–700 mL/d, and <1 suggests <1 L/d.
keyPoints: Fluid restriction is key in managing chronic hyponatremia.
Urine-to-plasma electrolyte ratio guides fluid restriction: >1 = <500 mL/d; ~1 = 500-700 mL/d; <1 = <1 L/d.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Hyponatremia

items.title: Use of AVP Antagonists (Vaptans) in SIAD and Hypervolemic Hyponatremia
items.content: AVP antagonists, such as tolvaptan and conivaptan, are effective for managing Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH Secretion (SIAD) and hypervolemic hyponatremia due to heart failure or cirrhosis. Tolvaptan is an oral V₂ antagonist approved by the FDA, while conivaptan is an intravenous mixed V₁/V₂ antagonist. Therapy should be initiated in a hospital with fluid restriction liberalization (>2 L/d) and close monitoring of plasma Na⁺ concentration.
keyPoints: Tolvaptan is an oral V₂ AVP receptor antagonist approved by the FDA.
Conivaptan is an intravenous mixed V₁/V₂ antagonist, with a risk of hypotension due to V₁A receptor inhibition.
Initiate therapy in a hospital setting with fluid restriction liberalization (>2 L/d) and close monitoring of plasma Na⁺ concentration.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · TABLE 56-3 Management of Hypernatremia

items.title: Management of Acute Symptomatic Hyponatremia with Hypertonic Saline
items.content: Acute symptomatic hyponatremia should be treated with hypertonic 3% saline (513 mM) to rapidly increase plasma Na⁺ concentration by 1–2 mM/h, aiming for a total increase of 4–6 mM. A bolus of 100 mL is more effective than an infusion for rapid improvement. Monitor plasma Na⁺ every 2–4 hours during treatment and adjust therapy as needed.
keyPoints: Use hypertonic 3% saline (513 mM) to acutely increase plasma Na⁺ concentration by 1–2 mM/h, aiming for a total of 4–6 mM.
A bolus of 100 mL is more effective than an infusion for rapid improvement.
Monitor plasma Na⁺ every 2–4 hours during treatment and adjust therapy as needed.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · TABLE 56-3 Management of Hypernatremia

items.title: Rate of Correction in Chronic Hyponatremia to Avoid ODS
items.content: In chronic hyponatremia, the rate of correction should be slow (<6–8 mM in the first 24 hours and <6 mM each subsequent 24 hours) to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS). Lower target rates are recommended for patients at higher risk for ODS. Overcorrection can occur if AVP levels normalize rapidly, such as after treatment with intravenous saline or glucocorticoid replacement.
keyPoints: Correct chronic hyponatremia slowly (<6–8 mM in the first 24 hours and <6 mM each subsequent 24 hours) to avoid ODS.
Lower target rates are recommended for patients at higher risk for ODS, such as alcoholics or hypokalemic patients.
Overcorrection can occur if AVP levels normalize rapidly after treatment with intravenous saline or glucocorticoid replacement.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · TABLE 56-3 Management of Hypernatremia

items.title: Etiology and Risk Factors for Hypernatremia
items.content: Hypernatremia is defined by a plasma Na⁺ concentration >145 mM, often resulting from water loss exceeding sodium loss. It can be caused by insensible losses due to fever or burns, gastrointestinal losses like diarrhea, renal losses such as osmotic diuresis, and central or nephrogenic diabetes insipidus (DI). Elderly individuals with reduced thirst are at higher risk.
keyPoints: Hypernatremia is defined by plasma Na⁺ >145 mM.
Mortality rates can be 40–60% due to underlying diseases.
Elderly individuals with reduced thirst are at highest risk.
Causes include insensible losses, diarrhea, osmotic diuresis, and DI.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPERNATREMIA (part 1)

items.title: Pathophysiology of Hypernatremia
items.content: Hypernatremia increases extracellular fluid (ECF) osmolality, causing water to move out of cells and leading to cellular shrinkage. This can result in neurological symptoms such as confusion or coma. Chronic hypernatremia leads to brain cell adaptation through organic osmolyte accumulation, reducing severe neurologic compromise.
keyPoints: Hypernatremia increases ECF osmolality, causing cellular water efflux and shrinkage.
Neurological symptoms range from confusion to coma.
Chronic hypernatremia leads to brain cell adaptation via organic osmolytes.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPERNATREMIA (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Features and Complications of Hypernatremia
items.content: The primary clinical manifestation of hypernatremia is altered mental status. Acute hypernatremia can cause brain cell shrinkage, potentially leading to hemorrhages or subdural hematomas, especially in pediatric patients. Chronic hypernatremia allows for cellular adaptation but poses risks during rapid correction, such as cerebral edema and seizures.
keyPoints: Altered mental status is the most frequent manifestation of hypernatremia.
Acute hypernatremia can lead to brain cell shrinkage and hemorrhages.
Rapid correction in chronic hypernatremia may cause cerebral edema and seizures.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPERNATREMIA (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosing Hypernatremia: Key Laboratory Findings
items.content: In hypernatremia, serum osmolality >295 mOsm/kg should prompt an increase in circulating AVP and the excretion of low volumes (<500 mL/d) of maximally concentrated urine (osmolality >800 mOsm/kg). If these findings are absent, an extrarenal source is likely responsible. Polyuric patients may show osmotic diuresis with daily solute excretion >750–1000 mOsm/d or a predominant water diuresis with hypotonic urine.
keyPoints: Serum osmolality >295 mOsm/kg
Expected response: increased AVP, urine volume <500 mL/d, urine osmolality >800 mOsm/kg
Extrarenal source if expected response absent
Polyuric patients may have daily solute excretion >750–1000 mOsm/d or hypotonic urine
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPERNATREMIA (part 2)

items.title: Differentiating Causes of Polyuria in Hypernatremia
items.content: To differentiate nephrogenic from central diabetes insipidus (DI) in hypernatremic patients, measure urinary osmolality response to DDAVP and circulating AVP levels. High serum AVP and copeptin with no significant increase in urine osmolality (>50% or >150 mOsm/kg) after DDAVP indicates nephrogenic DI. Central DI will show a reduction in AVP and copeptin post-DDAVP.
keyPoints: Measure urinary osmolality response to DDAVP
High serum AVP and copeptin with <50% or <150 mOsm/kg increase indicates nephrogenic DI
Central DI shows reduced AVP and copeptin after DDAVP
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPERNATREMIA (part 2)

items.title: Correction Rate and Risks for Hypernatremia
items.content: The correction of hypernatremia should be gradual to prevent cerebral edema, ideally correcting plasma Na+ concentration by no more than 10 mM/d. In severe cases (>160 mM), this may take longer than 48 hours. Recent evidence suggests rapid correction does not increase mortality or risk for seizures and cerebral edema in critically ill adults.
keyPoints: Correct hypernatremia slowly to avoid cerebral edema.
Plasma Na+ concentration should be corrected by no more than 10 mM/d.
In severe hypernatremia (>160 mM), correction may take longer than 48 hours.
Rapid correction in critically ill adults does not increase mortality or risk for seizures and cerebral edema.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Hypernatremia

items.title: Management of Hypernatremia with Free Water Replacement
items.content: Water should be administered orally, by nasogastric tube, or via dextrose-containing IV solutions like 5% dextrose (D₅W) to provide free water. In cases where blood pressure and clinical volume status allow, hypotonic saline solutions may be used initially. For ongoing loss of free water in conditions such as NDI or central DI, urinary electrolyte-free water clearance must be calculated.
keyPoints: Administer water orally, by nasogastric tube, or via 5% dextrose (D₅W) for free water replacement.
Monitor blood glucose when using dextrose-containing IV solutions.
Use hypotonic saline solutions initially if clinically appropriate.
Calculate urinary electrolyte-free water clearance to estimate ongoing free water loss in NDI or central DI.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Hypernatremia

items.title: Role of Kidney and ENaC in Potassium Homeostasis
items.content: The kidney is central to potassium homeostasis, primarily through its ability to reabsorb or secrete K⁺ based on the body's needs. The ENaC-dependent Na⁺ entry into principal cells creates a lumen-negative potential difference that drives passive K⁺ exit via apical K⁺ channels, crucial for distal K⁺ secretion.
keyPoints: Plasma K⁺ concentration is maintained between 3.5 and 5.0 mM.
Kidney plays a dominant role in potassium homeostasis by excreting ~90% of daily intake via urine.
ENaC-dependent Na⁺ entry generates lumen-negative potential difference, driving passive K⁺ exit through apical channels.
Distal tubular K⁺ secretion is mediated by ROMK and BK channels.
Decreased distal delivery of Na⁺ can lead to hyperkalemia; increased delivery enhances K⁺ secretion leading to hypokalemia.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · POTASSIUM DISORDERS

items.title: Impact of Aldosterone and Other Factors on Potassium Regulation
items.content: Aldosterone significantly influences potassium excretion by enhancing ENaC activity, which amplifies the driving force for K⁺ secretion. However, factors other than aldosterone also modulate renal K⁺ handling, such as potassium restriction activating aldosterone-independent distal reabsorption of filtered K⁺.
keyPoints: Aldosterone increases ENaC activity, enhancing K⁺ secretion across the luminal membrane.
Potassium excess and restriction have opposing effects on apical K⁺ channel density and activity independently of aldosterone.
Hypokalemia activates aldosterone-independent distal reabsorption of filtered K⁺ via H⁺/K⁺-ATPase in intercalated cells.
Plasma K⁺ concentration changes are not universal in disorders with altered aldosterone activity.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · POTASSIUM DISORDERS

items.title: Understanding the Impact of Mineralocorticoid Excess on Hypokalemia
items.content: Mineralocorticoid excess leads to hypokalemia by increasing renal potassium excretion. Conditions such as primary hyperaldosteronism and Cushing's syndrome are key contributors.
keyPoints: Hypokalemia is defined as a plasma K⁺ concentration of <3.5 mM.
Occurs in up to 20% of hospitalized patients.
Associated with a tenfold increase in in-hospital mortality due to adverse cardiac effects.
Causes include primary hyperaldosteronism, Cushing's syndrome, Bartter's syndrome, and Gitelman's syndrome.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPOKALEMIA | TABLE 56-4 Causes of Hypokalemia

items.title: Identifying Non-Renal Causes of Hypokalemia: Apparent Mineralocorticoid Excess
items.content: Apparent mineralocorticoid excess can cause hypokalemia through genetic or acquired mechanisms affecting enzyme activity, leading to increased renal potassium loss.
keyPoints: Hypokalemia is defined as a plasma K⁺ concentration of <3.5 mM.
Occurs in up to 20% of hospitalized patients.
Causes include genetic deficiency of $11\beta$-dehydrogenase-2 and inhibition by substances like glycyrrhetinic acid, licorice, and certain drugs.
Liddle's syndrome is a genetic condition causing activation of epithelial Na⁺ channels.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPOKALEMIA | TABLE 56-4 Causes of Hypokalemia

items.title: Redistributive Hypokalemia and Thyrotoxic Periodic Paralysis
items.content: Redistributive hypokalemia can occur due to increased cellular uptake of potassium, often seen in conditions like hyperthyroidism or with certain medications. In thyrotoxic periodic paralysis (TPP), patients experience profound hypokalemia accompanied by hypophosphatemia and hypomagnesemia.
keyPoints: Redistributive hypokalemia involves increased cellular uptake of K+ due to Na+/K+-ATPase activity.
Conditions like hyperthyroidism, acute myocardial infarction, and alcohol withdrawal can cause hypokalemia through this mechanism.
TPP is characterized by profound hypokalemia (often severe), hypophosphatemia, and hypomagnesemia.
High-dose propranolol (3 mg/kg) can rapidly reverse TPP-associated electrolyte abnormalities.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 1)

items.title: Causes of Redistributive Hypokalemia
items.content: Redistributive hypokalemia results from increased cellular uptake of potassium, often mediated by insulin, β2-adrenergic activity, thyroid hormone, and alkalosis. Exogenous factors like insulin therapy or β2 agonists can exacerbate this condition.
keyPoints: Insulin, β2-adrenergic activity, thyroid hormone, and alkalosis promote Na+/K+-ATPase-mediated cellular uptake of K+.
Exogenous insulin can cause iatrogenic hypokalemia in states like diabetic ketoacidosis.
β2 agonists (e.g., bronchodilators) are potent activators of cellular K+ uptake, leading to hypokalemia.
Hidden sympathomimetics and xanthine derivatives can also provoke unexpected hypokalemia.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 1)

items.title: Nonrenal Losses of Potassium
items.content: While direct gastric losses of potassium are minimal, conditions like diarrhea and certain gastrointestinal disorders can lead to significant hypokalemia through mechanisms such as secondary hyperaldosteronism.
keyPoints: Direct gastric losses from vomiting or nasogastric suctioning result in minimal K+ loss but cause hypochloremic alkalosis with persistent kaliuresis.
Diarrhea is a major global cause of hypokalemia due to significant potassium loss.
Noninfectious GI conditions like celiac disease and inflammatory bowel disease can lead to hypokalemia via upregulated colonic BK channels.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 1)

items.title: Thiazides vs Loop Diuretics in Hypokalemia
items.content: Thiazides are more likely to cause hypokalemia compared to loop diuretics due to their mechanism of action on the Na⁺-Cl⁻ cotransporter and associated changes in calcium handling.
keyPoints: Thiazides inhibit the Na⁺-Cl⁻ cotransporter NCC in DCT cells, increasing luminal Na⁺ delivery to principal cells downstream.
This leads to increased ENaC activity and a higher lumen-negative potential difference, amplifying K⁺ secretion.
Thiazide-associated hypocalciuria contrasts with loop diuretic-induced hypercalciuria, which inhibits ENaC and reduces distal K⁺ excretion.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 2)

items.title: Aldosterone's Role in Hypokalemia
items.content: Increases in aldosterone activity, whether primary or secondary, are associated with hypokalemia due to enhanced K⁺ excretion via ENaC activation.
keyPoints: Aldosterone activates the ENaC channel in principal cells, increasing the driving force for $K^{+}$ excretion.
Hyperaldosteronism can be primary (genetic or acquired) or secondary, with renal artery stenosis being a common cause of secondary hyperaldosteronism.
Primary hyperaldosteronism forms include familial hyperaldosteronism types I-III and acquired causes like aldosterone-producing adenomas.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 2)

items.title: Screening for Primary Hyperaldosteronism
items.content: The aldosterone:PRA ratio is a useful screening tool for primary hyperaldosteronism, with a ratio >50 being suggestive.
keyPoints: An aldosterone:PRA ratio of >50 suggests primary hyperaldosteronism in hypokalemic and/or hypertensive patients.
Hypokalemia and antihypertensive drugs can alter the aldosterone:PRA ratio, potentially leading to a false negative (<50).
Drugs affecting the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis should be discontinued during workup, except for verapamil, alpha blockers, hydralazine, and low-dose amiloride.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 2)

items.title: Mechanism of Hypokalemia in Apparent Mineralocorticoid Excess (SAME) due to 11βHSD-2 Dysfunction
items.content: In SAME, hypokalemia arises from cortisol-dependent activation of the mineralocorticoid receptor (MLR), mimicking aldosterone effects. This occurs when mutations or inhibitors reduce 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase-2 (11βHSD-2) activity, preventing conversion of cortisol to cortisone.
keyPoints: Cortisol activates MLR in the absence of 11βHSD-2 function, leading to mineralocorticoid-like effects.
Conditions include hypertension, hypokalemia, hypercalciuria, and metabolic alkalosis with suppressed plasma renin activity (PRA) and aldosterone.
Inhibitors such as glycyrrhetinic acid from licorice and certain antifungals can also cause SAME.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 3)

items.title: Hypokalemia in Cushing's Syndrome: Impact of ACTH Source
items.content: The incidence of hypokalemia varies significantly between Cushing's syndrome caused by pituitary ACTH and ectopic ACTH secretion, due to differences in renal 11βHSD-2 activity.
keyPoints: Cushing's syndrome from pituitary ACTH has a 10% incidence of hypokalemia.
Ectopic ACTH causes hypokalemia in 60–100% of patients, with reduced 11βHSD-2 activity contributing to SAME.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 3)

items.title: Magnesium Deficiency and Its Role in Hypokalemia
items.content: Magnesium depletion impairs muscle Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase activity, leading to secondary kaliuresis and increased K⁺ secretion by the distal nephron.
keyPoints: Hypomagnesemia reduces intracellular block of K⁺ efflux through ROMK channels in principal cells.
Magnesium repletion is necessary for effective potassium replacement therapy.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 3)

items.title: Hypokalemia and Cardiac Risks
items.content: Hypokalemia significantly impacts cardiac function, increasing the risk of ventricular and atrial arrhythmias. It exacerbates digoxin toxicity by reducing competition between potassium and digoxin for binding sites on Na+/K+-ATPase subunits.
keyPoints: Hypokalemia is a major risk factor for ventricular and atrial arrhythmias.
It predisposes to digoxin toxicity through reduced competition with K+ at cardiac Na+/K+-ATPase binding sites.
ECG changes include broad flat T waves, ST depression, and QT prolongation, especially when serum K+ < 2.7 mmol/L.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 4)

items.title: Hypokalemia and Renal Effects
items.content: Hypokalemia affects renal function by causing Na+-Cl- and HCO3- retention, polyuria, and other structural changes in the kidney. It can lead to acute kidney injury and end-stage renal disease (ESRD) with prolonged exposure.
keyPoints: Causes Na+-Cl- and HCO3- retention, contributing to metabolic alkalosis.
Leads to hypokalemic polyuria due to central polydipsia and AVP-resistant renal concentrating defect.
Structural kidney changes include vacuolizing injury to proximal tubular cells, interstitial nephritis, and renal cysts.
Can progress to acute kidney injury and ESRD with chronic exposure.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 4)

items.title: Diagnosing Hypokalemia
items.content: The diagnosis of hypokalemia involves evaluating history, physical examination, and laboratory tests. Key lab findings include electrolytes, BUN, creatinine, serum osmolality, Mg2+, Ca2+, complete blood count, and urinary pH/osmolality/creatinine/electrolytes.
keyPoints: History should focus on medications (e.g., diuretics), diet, and symptoms like periodic weakness or diarrhea.
Physical exam should assess blood pressure, volume status, and signs of specific disorders (e.g., hyperthyroidism).
Initial labs include electrolytes, BUN, creatinine, serum osmolality, Mg2+, Ca2+, CBC, and urinary pH/osmolality/creatinine/electrolytes.
Non-anion gap acidosis suggests distal renal tubular acidosis or diarrhea; urinary anion gap aids differentiation.
24-h urine K+ excretion < 15 mmol indicates extrarenal hypokalemia cause.
TTKG < 3 in hypokalemia, or urinary K+-to-creatinine ratio >13 mmol/g creatinine suggests excessive renal K+ excretion.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 4)

items.title: Identifying Hyperaldosteronism in Young Patients
items.content: In patients under 20 years old or with a family history of primary aldosteronism or early stroke, testing for the chimeric FH-I/GRA gene is recommended if hyperaldosteronism is suspected.
keyPoints: Plasma aldosterone:PRA ratio >50 suggests hyperaldosteronism.
Test for chimeric FH-I/GRA gene in patients <20 years or with family history of primary aldosteronism/stroke under 40.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 5) | Hypokalemia

items.title: Differentiating Liddle's Syndrome from SAME
items.content: Liddle's syndrome and SAME both cause hypokalemia and hypertension with aldosterone suppression, but they respond differently to medications.
keyPoints: Liddle's syndrome responds to amiloride (ENaC inhibition) but not spironolactone.
SAME responds to spironolactone.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 5) | Hypokalemia

items.title: Managing Severe Hypokalemia with Intravenous Therapy
items.content: Intravenous K⁺-Cl⁻ is used for severe hypokalemia, administered in saline solutions to avoid exacerbating hypokalemia.
keyPoints: Use intravenous K⁺-Cl⁻ in saline, not dextrose, to prevent worsening hypokalemia.
Peripheral IV dose: 20–40 mmol/L; central vein administration at 10–20 mmol/h with cardiac monitoring for severe cases.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 5) | Hypokalemia

items.title: Understanding Pseudohyperkalemia: Causes and Implications
items.content: Pseudohyperkalemia is a laboratory artifact where plasma potassium levels appear elevated due to factors unrelated to the body's actual potassium balance. It occurs because of increased cellular efflux or in vitro hemolysis, often seen with conditions like thrombocytosis, erythrocytosis, and leukocytosis.
keyPoints: Pseudohyperkalemia results from laboratory artifacts rather than true hyperkalemia.
Causes include cellular efflux due to thrombocytosis, erythrocytosis, leukocytosis, and in vitro hemolysis.
Hereditary defects in red cell membrane transport can also contribute.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPERKALEMIA | I. Pseudohyperkalemia

items.title: Differentiating True Hyperkalemia from Pseudohyperkalemia
items.content: True hyperkalemia is defined by a plasma potassium level of 5.5 mM or higher, whereas pseudohyperkalemia is an artifact caused by factors such as cellular efflux and in vitro hemolysis. Recognizing these differences is crucial for appropriate management.
keyPoints: Hyperkalemia is clinically significant when plasma potassium exceeds 5.5 mM.
Severe hyperkalemia (>6.0 mM) occurs in ~1% of hospitalized patients, with increased mortality risk.
Pseudohyperkalemia should be suspected in the presence of thrombocytosis, erythrocytosis, leukocytosis, or in vitro hemolysis.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPERKALEMIA | I. Pseudohyperkalemia

items.title: Risk of Hyperkalemia with Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone Axis Inhibition
items.content: Inhibition of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis, through medications like ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and mineralocorticoid receptor blockers (e.g., spironolactone), increases the risk of hyperkalemia. This is particularly significant when these drugs are used in combination.
keyPoints: ACE inhibitors, renin inhibitors, and ARBs inhibit the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis.
Risk of hyperkalemia is increased with combined use of these medications.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · III. Inadequate excretion (part 1)

items.title: Causes and Mechanisms of Hyperkalemia
items.content: Hyperkalemia can be caused by inadequate renal excretion, redistribution mechanisms, or excess intake/tissue necrosis. Key causes include drugs affecting the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis, acidosis (except anion gap), hypertonic solutions, and certain medications like digoxin.
keyPoints: Drugs impacting the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis are major hyperkalemia causes.
Redistribution mechanisms include acidemia (non-anion gap) and cationic amino acids.
Digoxin inhibits Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase, leading to impaired K⁺ uptake and hyperkalemia.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · III. Inadequate excretion (part 1)

items.title: Succinylcholine and Hyperkalemia Risk
items.content: Succinylcholine can cause acute hyperkalemia by depolarizing muscle cells, leading to K⁺ efflux through acetylcholine receptors. It is contraindicated in patients with conditions that increase AChR density at the plasma membrane.
keyPoints: Succinylcholine causes K⁺ efflux via acetylcholine receptor depolarization.
Contraindications include thermal trauma, neuromuscular injury, and prolonged immobilization.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · III. Inadequate excretion (part 1)

items.title: Hyperkalemia in Hyporenemic Hypoaldosteronism
items.content: Hyporenemic hypoaldosteronism is a common cause of hyperkalemia, particularly affecting diabetics, the elderly, and those with renal insufficiency. It involves suppressed plasma renin activity (PRA) and aldosterone levels.
keyPoints: Classically, patients have suppressed PRA and aldosterone.
~50% of these patients present with an associated acidosis characterized by a reduced renal excretion of NH₄⁺, a positive urinary anion gap, and urine pH <5.5.
Patients are often volume expanded, leading to increased circulating atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP) which inhibits both renal renin release and adrenal aldosterone release.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · III. Inadequate excretion (part 2)

items.title: Hyperkalemia in Renal Disease
items.content: Chronic kidney disease, especially end-stage kidney disease, is a prevalent cause of hyperkalemia due to reduced functioning nephrons.
keyPoints: Hyperkalemia is more common in oliguric acute kidney injury.
In non-oliguric patients, distal tubular flow rate and Na⁺ delivery are less limiting factors.
Tubulointerstitial diseases affecting the distal nephron (e.g., amyloidosis, sickle cell anemia) can cause hyperkalemia disproportionate to GFR.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · III. Inadequate excretion (part 2)

items.title: Medication-Associated Hyperkalemia
items.content: Several medications inhibit components of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis, leading to hyperkalemia.
keyPoints: ACE inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers, renin inhibitors, and mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (MLRs) are common culprits.
Yasmin-28 contains drospirenone, an MLR inhibitor that can cause hyperkalemia in susceptible patients.
Cyclosporine, tacrolimus, NSAIDs, and COX2 inhibitors induce hyporenemic hypoaldosteronism through multiple mechanisms.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · III. Inadequate excretion (part 2)

items.title: Hyperkalemia: Mechanisms and Emergency Management
items.content: Inhibition of ENaC by K+ sparing diuretics like amiloride can lead to hyperkalemia, which is a medical emergency due to its cardiac effects. Hyperkalemia affects the repolarization phase of the cardiac action potential, leading to characteristic ECG changes and potentially life-threatening arrhythmias.
keyPoints: Amiloride inhibits apical ENaC activity in the distal nephron, causing hyperkalemia.
Hyperkalemia can lead to sinus bradycardia, ventricular tachycardia, or asystole.
ECG changes: Tall peaked T waves (5.5–6.5 mM), loss of P waves (6.5–7.5 mM), widened QRS complex (7.0–8.0 mM), sine wave pattern (>8.0 mM).
Hyperkalemia can cause a type I Brugada pattern, differentiated from genetic Brugada's syndrome by absence of P waves and marked QRS widening.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · III. Inadequate excretion (part 3)

items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Hyperkalemia
items.content: The management of hyperkalemia involves assessing the need for emergency treatment and determining the underlying cause through history, physical examination, and laboratory tests.
keyPoints: Initial assessment includes medications, diet, kidney failure risk factors, urine output, blood pressure, and volume status.
Laboratory tests: electrolytes, BUN, creatinine, serum osmolality, Mg2+, Ca2+, complete blood count, urinary pH, osmolality, creatinine, and electrolytes.
Urine Na+ concentration <20 mM suggests distal Na+ delivery is limiting K+ excretion; volume repletion or furosemide may help reduce plasma K+.
TTKG calculation: {[K^{+}]_{urine}x Osm_{serum}}{[K^{+}]_{serum}x Osm_{urine}}, with expected values <3 in hypokalemia and >7–8 in hyperkalemia.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · III. Inadequate excretion (part 3)

items.title: Hyperkalemia-Induced Metabolic Acidosis
items.content: Hyperkalemia impairs the kidney's ability to excrete an acid load, contributing to metabolic acidosis due to competition between K+ and NH4+ for reabsorption.
keyPoints: Hyperkalemia competes with NH4+ for reabsorption by TALH, reducing medullary gradient for NH3/NH4 excretion.
Restoration of normokalemia can often correct hyperkalemic metabolic acidosis.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · III. Inadequate excretion (part 3)

items.title: Urgency in Treating Hyperkalemia with ECG Changes
items.content: ECG manifestations of hyperkalemia are a medical emergency requiring urgent treatment. Immediate management includes hospital admission, continuous cardiac monitoring, and intravenous calcium to protect the heart.
keyPoints: Plasma K⁺ concentration ≥6.5 mM is significant even without ECG changes.
Intravenous calcium (10 mL of 10% calcium gluconate) raises action potential threshold, reducing excitability.
Calcium infusion starts in 1–3 min and lasts 30–60 min; repeat if no change or recurrence in ECG findings.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Hyperkalemia (part 1)

items.title: Rapid Reduction of Plasma K⁺ with Insulin
items.content: Insulin, combined with glucose, is used to rapidly reduce plasma potassium by shifting it into cells. This treatment must be carefully monitored due to the risk of hypoglycemia.
keyPoints: Recommended dose: 10 units of intravenous regular insulin followed by 50 mL of 50% dextrose (D₅₀W).
Effect begins in 10–20 min, peaks at 30–60 min, and lasts for 4–6 h.
Monitor plasma glucose closely; follow with 10% dextrose infusion if needed.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Hyperkalemia (part 1)

items.title: Use of β₂-Agonists in Hyperkalemia Management
items.content: β₂-agonists like albuterol are effective for acute hyperkalemia management, especially when used with insulin. They should be used cautiously in patients with cardiac disease.
keyPoints: Recommended dose: 10–20 mg of nebulized albuterol over 10 min.
Effect starts at about 30 min, peaks at 90 min, lasts for 2–6 h.
~20% of ESRD patients are resistant to β₂-agonists; use with insulin.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Hyperkalemia (part 1)

items.title: Management of Hyperkalemia with Potassium Binders
items.content: Patiromer and sodium zirconium cyclosilicate are preferred over SPS for managing hyperkalemia due to their lack of intestinal toxicity. Patiromer binds K⁺ in exchange for Ca²⁺, while sodium zirconium cyclosilicate exchanges Na⁺ and H⁺ ions for K⁺ and NH₄⁺.
keyPoints: Patiromer is a nonabsorbed polymer that decreases urinary potassium, magnesium, and sodium excretion.
A major side effect of patiromer is hypomagnesemia.
Sodium zirconium cyclosilicate exchanges Na⁺ and H⁺ ions for K⁺ and NH₄⁺ in the intestine.
These agents allow greater intensity of renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibition.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Hyperkalemia (part 2)

items.title: Hemodialysis as a Treatment for Hyperkalemia
items.content: Hemodialysis is the most effective method to reduce plasma K⁺ concentration, especially in patients with acute kidney injury or ESRD. The effectiveness depends on factors like the distribution of K⁺ between ICF and ECF, dialyzer type, flow rates, and dialysate-to-plasma K⁺ gradient.
keyPoints: Hemodialysis is more effective than peritoneal dialysis for reducing plasma K⁺ concentration.
Effectiveness depends on the relative distribution of K⁺ between ICF and ECF.
Factors influencing effectiveness include dialyzer type, surface area, flow rates, and dialysate-to-plasma K⁺ gradient.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Hyperkalemia (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following mechanisms is primarily responsible for increasing interstitial osmolality in the inner medulla of the kidney?
options: AVP-induced activation of Na+ -Cl- transport by the thick ascending limb of Henle
Passive absorption of water across aquaporin-1 channels in the descending thin limb of Henle
Active reabsorption of Na+ via ENaC channels in principal cells of the collecting duct
Urea transport in the renal medulla
items.explanation: AVP-induced activation of Na+-Cl- transport by the thick ascending limb is a key participant in increasing interstitial osmolality.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances (part 1)

items.question: Under antidiuretic conditions, what happens to urine osmolarity when AVP levels are increased?
options: Urine osmolarity increases, resulting in concentrated urine
items.explanation: Increased AVP levels lead to reabsorption of water, increasing urine osmolarity and resulting in concentrated urine.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary role of aldosterone in renal Na+ handling?
options: Activation of proximal tubule Na+-Cl- reabsorption
Stimulation of aquaporin-2 insertion in collecting ducts
Induction of Na+ absorption and K+ excretion via ENaC channels
Inhibition of Na+ reabsorption in the thick ascending limb
items.explanation: Aldosterone activates ENaC channels, leading to Na+ absorption and promoting K+ excretion.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances (part 1)

items.question: Which segment of the nephron is primarily responsible for reabsorbing approximately two-thirds of filtered Na+-Cl-?
items.explanation: The proximal tubule reabsorbs approximately two-thirds of filtered Na+-Cl-.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances (part 1)

items.question: Which neurohumoral response is activated by the 'underfilling' of the arterial circulation?
options: Increased sympathetic tone, activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis, increased circulating AVP
Decreased sympathetic tone, inhibition of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis, decreased circulating AVP
Activation of parasympathetic nervous system, increased circulating dopamine
Inhibition of the sympathetic nervous system, activation of natriuretic peptides
items.explanation: Underfilling triggers neurohumoral responses to increase vascular resistance and renal reabsorption.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances (part 2)

items.question: What is a common consequence of decreased oncotic pressure in the context of fluid disturbances?
items.explanation: Decreased oncotic pressure contributes to underfilling by reducing fluid retention in vessels.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a function of AVP in defending circulatory integrity?
options: Decreasing renal retention of water and Na⁺-Cl⁻
items.explanation: AVP increases, not decreases, renal retention of water and Na⁺-Cl⁻.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following conditions can lead to osmotic diuresis due to impaired tubular reabsorption of Na⁺-Cl⁻ and water?
options: A high filtered load of endogenous solutes such as glucose and urea
Excessive sweating without adequate repletion of electrolytes
items.explanation: A high filtered load of endogenous solutes like glucose and urea can impair tubular reabsorption, causing osmotic diuresis.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPOVOLEMIA (part 1)

items.question: Which drug inhibits proximal tubular Na⁺-Cl⁻ absorption via inhibition of carbonic anhydrase?
items.explanation: Acetazolamide inhibits proximal tubular Na⁺-Cl⁻ absorption by inhibiting carbonic anhydrase.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPOVOLEMIA (part 1)

items.question: Which condition is associated with reduced reabsorption of filtered Na⁺-Cl⁻ and/or water due to tubulointerstitial injury?
items.explanation: Tubulointerstitial injuries, such as interstitial nephritis, can reduce distal tubular Na⁺-Cl⁻ and/or water absorption.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPOVOLEMIA (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a reliable sign of hypovolemia?
items.explanation: Orthostatic tachycardia is a reliable sign of hypovolemia.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPOVOLEMIA (part 1)

items.question: Which laboratory finding is more dependable for measuring GFR in hypovolemic patients?
items.explanation: Creatinine is the more dependable measure of GFR because BUN levels can be influenced by various factors.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPOVOLEMIA (part 2) | Hypovolemia

items.question: In nonrenal causes of hypovolemia, what is the typical urine Na⁺ concentration?
items.explanation: The urine Na⁺ concentration is typically <20 mM in nonrenal causes of hypovolemia.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPOVOLEMIA (part 2) | Hypovolemia

items.question: Which solution is most appropriate for resuscitation in normonatremic or hyponatremic patients with severe hypovolemia?
options: Colloid solutions such as intravenous albumin
items.explanation: Isotonic, 'normal' saline (0.9% NaCl) is the most appropriate resuscitation fluid for normonatremic or hyponatremic patients with severe hypovolemia.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPOVOLEMIA (part 2) | Hypovolemia

items.question: Which two key effectors are primarily involved in defending serum osmolality?
items.explanation: Water intake and circulating AVP are the primary effectors in defending serum osmolality.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · SODIUM DISORDERS

items.question: What primarily causes most cases of hyponatremia and hypernatremia?
options: Defects in water intake or AVP
items.explanation: Most cases of hyponatremia and hypernatremia are caused by defects in water intake or AVP.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · SODIUM DISORDERS

items.question: What does the absolute plasma Na⁺ concentration indicate about a patient's volume status?
options: It tells nothing about volume status.
items.explanation: The absolute plasma Na⁺ concentration tells one nothing about the patient's volume status.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · SODIUM DISORDERS

items.question: Which urine Na⁺ concentration typically indicates nonrenal causes of hypovolemic hyponatremia?
items.explanation: Nonrenal causes of hypovolemic hyponatremia are indicated by a urine Na⁺ concentration typically <20 mM.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 1)

items.question: What is the typical urine Na⁺ concentration in renal causes of hypovolemic hyponatremia?
items.explanation: Renal causes of hypovolemic hyponatremia typically have a urine Na⁺ concentration >20 mM.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 1)

items.question: Which condition is associated with high urine Na⁺ concentration and hyperkalemia in hypovolemic patients?
items.explanation: Hyperkalemia and high urine Na⁺ concentration in a hypovolemic patient suggest primary adrenal insufficiency.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 1)

items.question: Which diuretic is less frequently associated with hyponatremia due to its effect on the renal concentrating mechanism?
items.explanation: Loop diuretics are less frequently associated with hyponatremia because they inhibit Na⁺-Cl⁻ and K⁺ absorption, reducing urine concentration ability.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 1)

items.question: Which condition is most frequently associated with euvolemic hyponatremia?
items.explanation: SIAD (Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone secretion) is the most frequent cause of euvolemic hyponatremia.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 2)

items.question: What is a common feature in patients with SIAD regarding serum uric acid levels?
options: Serum uric acid is often high (>4 mg/dL)
Serum uric acid is often low (<4 mg/dL)
Serum uric acid levels are unaffected
Serum uric acid levels fluctuate widely
items.explanation: Patients with SIAD often have low serum uric acid (<4 mg/dL) due to suppressed proximal tubular transport.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is a recognized pattern of AVP secretion in patients with SIAD?
options: AVP suppression at higher serum osmolalities
items.explanation: Patients with SIAD can have unregulated, erratic AVP secretion or fail to suppress AVP at lower serum osmolalities; some may have no detectable circulating AVP.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 2)

items.question: Which condition is associated with a gain-in-function mutation in the V2 AVP receptor?
items.explanation: Gain-in-function mutations of the V2 AVP receptor can lead to nephrogenic SIAD.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is a key complication of acute hyponatremia?
items.explanation: Acute hyponatremia can lead to normocapneic or hypercapneic respiratory failure due to noncardiogenic, neurogenic pulmonary edema.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 3)

items.question: What is the primary cause of acute hyponatremic encephalopathy?
options: Rapid increase in plasma Na⁺ concentration
Overwhelmed volume regulatory mechanisms due to rapid decrease in tonicity
items.explanation: Acute hyponatremic encephalopathy occurs when volume regulatory mechanisms are overwhelmed by a rapid decrease in tonicity, leading to acute cerebral edema.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following is a risk factor for osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS) during correction of hyponatremia?
options: Slow and controlled increase in plasma Na⁺ concentration
items.explanation: Overly rapid correction of hyponatremia can lead to osmotic demyelination syndrome due to hypertonic stress in astrocytes.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 3)

items.question: What is a common clinical presentation of central pontine myelinolysis?
options: Para-paresis or quadriparesis, dysphagia, and dysarthria
items.explanation: Central pontine myelinolysis can present with para-paresis or quadriparesis, dysphagia, and dysarthria after overcorrection of hyponatremia.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 3)

items.question: Which diagnostic test should be considered in patients at high risk for small-cell carcinoma of the lung when assessing hyponatremia?
items.explanation: CT scanning of the thorax should be considered in patients at high risk for small-cell carcinoma of the lung.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 4)

items.question: What is a potential cause of pseudohyponatremia?
items.explanation: Pseudohyponatremia can occur due to extreme hyperlipidemia and/or hyperproteinemia.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 4)

items.question: What urine Na⁺ concentration is consistent with hypovolemic hyponatremia?
items.explanation: A urine Na⁺ concentration <20–30 mM is consistent with hypovolemic hyponatremia.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 4)

items.question: Which condition can cause a plasma Na⁺ concentration to fall by ~1.6–2.4 mM for every 100-mg/dL increase in glucose?
items.explanation: Plasma Na⁺ concentration falls by ~1.6–2.4 mM for every 100-mg/dL increase in glucose due to hyperglycemia.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPONATREMIA (part 4)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a major consideration for the therapy of hyponatremia?
options: The urgency and goals of therapy based on symptoms.
Risk assessment for ODS with rapid correction of plasma Na⁺ concentration.
Response to interventions like hypertonic saline, isotonic saline, or AVP antagonists.
Immediate administration of potassium supplements in all cases.
items.explanation: The passage does not mention immediate administration of potassium supplements as a major consideration for therapy; it discusses the need based on specific conditions like hypokalemia.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Hyponatremia

items.question: Which type of hyponatremia may respond to improved therapy of underlying cardiomyopathy?
items.explanation: The passage states that hypervolemic hyponatremia due to CHF often responds to improved therapy of the underlying cardiomyopathy, such as ACE inhibition.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Hyponatremia

items.question: In patients with SIAD, what is a recommended initial pharmacologic therapy if fluid restriction and increased solute intake fail?
options: Oral furosemide combined with oral salt tablets.
items.explanation: The passage suggests that some patients with SIAD initially respond to therapy combining oral furosemide and salt tablets if other treatments fail.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Hyponatremia

items.question: Which condition is associated with a very high risk of developing ODS due to rapid correction?
items.explanation: The passage notes that patients with beer potomania have a very high risk of developing ODS due to factors like hypokalemia and the potential for overcorrection of plasma Na⁺ concentration.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Hyponatremia

items.question: Which AVP antagonist is approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration for oral use?
items.explanation: Tolvaptan is currently the only oral V₂ antagonist approved by the U.S. FDA.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · TABLE 56-3 Management of Hypernatremia

items.question: What is a potential risk associated with intravenous vaptans?
items.explanation: Conivaptan, the only available IV vaptan, has a modest risk of hypotension due to V₁A receptor inhibition.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · TABLE 56-3 Management of Hypernatremia

items.question: In managing acute symptomatic hyponatremia, what is the initial plasma Na⁺ concentration increase goal per hour?
items.explanation: Hypertonic saline is used to acutely increase plasma Na⁺ concentration by 1–2 mM/h.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · TABLE 56-3 Management of Hypernatremia

items.question: Which treatment option is not approved for acute hyponatremia?
items.explanation: AVP antagonists do not have an approved role in the management of acute hyponatremia.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · TABLE 56-3 Management of Hypernatremia

items.question: Which condition is most commonly associated with hypernatremia due to gastrointestinal causes?
items.explanation: Diarrhea is the most common gastrointestinal cause of hypernatremia, with osmotic diarrhea typically generating stools with low sodium and potassium levels.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPERNATREMIA (part 1)

items.question: What is a common acquired cause of nephrogenic diabetes insipidus (NDI) that inhibits the renal response to AVP?
items.explanation: Hypokalemia is a common acquired cause of NDI, as it inhibits the renal response to AVP and downregulates aquaporin-2 expression.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPERNATREMIA (part 1)

items.question: Which drug can cause nephrogenic diabetes insipidus by directly inhibiting renal glycogen synthase kinase-3?
items.explanation: Lithium causes NDI by multiple mechanisms, including direct inhibition of renal glycogen synthase kinase-3.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPERNATREMIA (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary mechanism through which hypercalcemia reduces renal concentrating ability?
options: Downregulates Na+, K+, and Cl- transport by the TALH
Activates membrane transporters for organic osmolytics
items.explanation: Hypercalcemia reduces renal concentrating ability by downregulating Na+, K+, and Cl- transport by the TALH.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPERNATREMIA (part 1)

items.question: Which laboratory finding is indicative of an extrarenal source primarily responsible for hypernatremia?
options: Urine osmolality >800 mOsm/kg with low urine volume (<500 mL/d)
Urine osmolality <300 mOsm/kg with high urine volume
Serum copeptin levels decrease after hypertonic saline infusion
items.explanation: An extrarenal source of water loss is indicated by maximally concentrated urine (osmolality >800 mOsm/kg) and low volumes (<500 mL/d).
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPERNATREMIA (part 2)

items.question: What does a partial response to DDAVP in hypernatremic patients indicate?
options: Central diabetes insipidus with high AVP levels
Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus with low AVP levels
items.explanation: A partial response to DDAVP, with a >50% rise in urine osmolality that does not reach 800 mOsm/kg, suggests partial nephrogenic diabetes insipidus.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPERNATREMIA (part 2)

items.question: Why is water deprivation contraindicated in patients with hypernatremia?
options: It can lead to hypovolemia and orthostasis
It causes an increase in serum copeptin levels
It results in a decrease in circulating AVP
items.explanation: Water deprivation is contraindicated because it can exacerbate hypernatremia.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPERNATREMIA (part 2)

items.question: What is the recommended maximum rate for correcting plasma Na+ concentration in adults with hypernatremia to avoid cerebral edema?
items.explanation: The recommended maximum rate is no more than 10 mM/d.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Hypernatremia

items.question: Which IV solution can be used to administer free water in the treatment of hypernatremia?
items.explanation: 5% dextrose (D₅W) can be used to administer free water.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Hypernatremia

items.question: In the management of hypernatremia, which medication is appropriate for reducing polyuria in patients with NDI due to lithium?
items.explanation: Amiloride can reduce polyuria by decreasing entry of lithium into principal cells.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · Hypernatremia

items.question: Which part of the nephron is primarily responsible for renal K⁺ secretion?
options: Principal cells of the connecting segment (CNT) and cortical CD
items.explanation: The principal cells of the connecting segment (CNT) and cortical collecting duct play a dominant role in renal K⁺ secretion.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · POTASSIUM DISORDERS

items.question: What is the primary mechanism by which aldosterone influences potassium excretion?
options: Inhibits ENaC channels, reducing Na⁺ entry and thus K⁺ secretion
Increases activity of apical H⁺/K⁺-ATPase in intercalated cells
Activates ENaC channels, increasing Na⁺ entry and driving K⁺ secretion
items.explanation: Aldosterone increases the activity of ENaC channels, which generates a lumen-negative potential difference that drives K⁺ secretion.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · POTASSIUM DISORDERS

items.question: Which condition can lead to hyperkalemia due to reduced excretion of potassium?
options: Increased distal delivery of Na⁺ following loop diuretics
Acute kidney injury with massive tissue necrosis
items.explanation: Massive necrosis and acute kidney injury can lead to severe hyperkalemia due to reduced excretion of K⁺.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · POTASSIUM DISORDERS

items.question: Which condition is NOT listed as a cause of hypokalemia due to mineralocorticoid excess?
items.explanation: Hypovolemia is listed under secondary hyperaldosteronism, not mineralocorticoid excess.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPOKALEMIA | TABLE 56-4 Causes of Hypokalemia

items.question: Which syndrome involves genetic activation of epithelial Na⁺ channels as a cause of hypokalemia?
items.explanation: Liddle's syndrome involves genetic activation of epithelial Na⁺ channels.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPOKALEMIA | TABLE 56-4 Causes of Hypokalemia

items.question: Which substance is NOT mentioned as causing inhibition of 11β-dehydrogenase-2 leading to apparent mineralocorticoid excess?
items.explanation: Glycyrrhetinic acid is mentioned, but not glycyrrhetinic acid specifically.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · HYPOKALEMIA | TABLE 56-4 Causes of Hypokalemia

items.question: Which condition can occasionally cause spurious hypokalemia due to in vitro cellular uptake of potassium after venipuncture?
items.explanation: Spurious hypokalemia can result from in vitro cellular uptake of potassium due to profound leukocytosis in acute leukemia.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 1)

items.question: What is a common cause of redistributive hypokalemia during the management of K+ deficient states?
items.explanation: Exogenous insulin can cause iatrogenic hypokalemia during the management of K+ deficient states.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 1)

items.question: Which genetic variation has been linked to a predisposition for thyrotoxic periodic paralysis?
options: Missense mutations of voltage sensor domains within the α1 subunit of L-type calcium channels
items.explanation: Genetic variation in Kir2.6 has been linked to a predisposition for thyrotoxic periodic paralysis.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 1)

items.question: What is the role of high-dose propranolol in patients with thyrotoxic periodic paralysis?
options: It increases potassium uptake by muscle
It rapidly reverses hypokalemia, hypophosphatemia, and paralysis
It enhances outward-directed inward-rectifying K+ current
items.explanation: High-dose propranolol rapidly reverses the associated hypokalemia, hypophosphatemia, and paralysis in TPP.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 1)

items.question: Which class of diuretics is more likely to cause hypokalemia due to their effect on plasma K⁺ concentration?
items.explanation: Thiazides have a greater effect on plasma K⁺ concentration than loop diuretics, leading to more hypokalemia.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 2)

items.question: What is the mechanism by which thiazide diuretics increase distal tubular Na⁺ delivery and K⁺ secretion?
options: Inhibition of ENaC in principal cells
Inhibition of the Na⁺-Cl⁻ cotransporter NCC in DCT cells
items.explanation: Thiazides inhibit the Na⁺-Cl⁻ cotransporter NCC in DCT cells, increasing luminal Na⁺ delivery to principal cells and K⁺ secretion.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 2)

items.question: Which genetic mutation is associated with familial hyperaldosteronism type III (FH-III)?
items.explanation: FH-III is caused by mutations in the KCNJ5 gene, leading to sodium permeability and aldosterone secretion.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 2)

items.question: What condition may result from acquired somatic mutations in KCNJ5 or ATP1A1 genes?
items.explanation: Acquired somatic mutations in KCNJ5 or ATP1A1 genes can lead to primary hyperaldosteronism.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 2)

items.question: Which enzyme protects cells in the aldosterone-sensitive distal nephron from cortisol's mineralocorticoid-like activity?
items.explanation: The enzyme 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase-2 converts cortisol to cortisone, preventing its activation of the mineralocorticoid receptor.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 3)

items.question: What is a common cause of apparent mineralocorticoid excess (SAME) due to biochemical inhibition?
items.explanation: Glycyrrhetinic acid, found in licorice root, inhibits 11βHSD-2 and can cause SAME.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 3)

items.question: What is the effect of magnesium depletion on muscle Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase activity?
options: It has no effect on ATPase activity
It converts ATPase to an inactive form
items.explanation: Magnesium depletion inhibits muscle Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase activity, reducing potassium influx into cells.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 3)

items.question: Which syndrome is characterized by autosomal dominant gain-of-function mutations of ENaC subunits?
items.explanation: Liddle's syndrome is caused by gain-of-function mutations in ENaC subunits, leading to increased sodium reabsorption.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following is a major risk factor for both ventricular and atrial arrhythmias in hypokalemia?
items.explanation: Hypokalemia has prominent effects on cardiac muscle cells and is a major risk factor for arrhythmias.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 4)

items.question: What ECG change is most marked when serum potassium is <2.7 mmol/L in hypokalemia?
options: Broad flat T waves and QT prolongation
items.explanation: Hypokalemia causes broad flat T waves, ST depression, and QT prolongation, especially when serum potassium is <2.7 mmol/L.
items.reference: Fluid and Electrolyte Disturbances · 3. Magnesium deficiency (part 4)
